pi_items.source: Task setup script confirms this directory exists and file is created at task start
Verifier checks for minimum file size of 500 bytes; reasonable threshold for comprehensive first aid documentation
CAMEO Chemicals NOAA database contains entry for Hydrofluoric Acid (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/2013)
Confirmed on CAMEO Chemicals datasheet; standard chemical name identifier
Common chemical abbreviation for Hydrofluoric Acid; verified in safety documentation
CAMEO Chemicals explicitly mentions 'calcium gluconate gel' as treatment for hydrofluoric acid skin contact
CAMEO Chemicals states: 'if calcium gluconate gel is available, rinse 5 minutes, then apply gel'
CAMEO Chemicals NOAA database contains entry for Phenol (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/1310)

Institutional safety guidelines (Cornell EHS, University of York, etc.) recommend polyethylene glycol PEG-300 or PEG-400 as first aid treatment for phenol; however, CAMEO Chemicals datasheet primarily recommends water flushing and does not explicitly mention polyethylene glycol
Confirmed in institutional safety protocols as standard treatment (polyethylene glycol); not explicitly stated on CAMEO Chemicals phenol page
PEG (polyethylene glycol) is the standard abbreviation used in safety literature for phenol first aid; not explicitly mentioned on CAMEO Chemicals page
Some institutional guidelines mention isopropyl alcohol as an alternative if water is available; not found on CAMEO Chemicals phenol page
Institutional guidelines (Cornell, University of York) mention glycerol as an alternative treatment when PEG is unavailable; not found on CAMEO Chemicals phenol page
CAMEO Chemicals NOAA database contains entry for Sodium Cyanide (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/4477)

CAMEO Chemicals treatment guidance states 'the victim should breathe amyl nitrite, with a nitrite pearl broken in a cloth and held under the victim's nose'; however, amyl nitrite is no longer approved by FDA as a standalone treatment (as of 2018)
CAMEO Chemicals acknowledges need for antidote administration; specific antidotes include hydroxocobalamin (preferred), sodium nitrite, and sodium thiosulfate
CAMEO Chemicals states 'have victim drink 1% solution of sodium thiosulfate to be repeated in 15 minutes'
Sodium nitrite and amyl nitrite contain nitrite; both are mentioned in cyanide treatment protocols

Hydroxocobalamin is the current FDA-approved first-line antidote for cyanide poisoning (CYANOKIT); designated as preferred by expert consensus since 2018; may not be explicitly mentioned on older CAMEO pages but is authoritative current medical treatment
All three chemicals have inhalation exposure routes described in safety guidelines and CAMEO materials
All three chemicals have dermal (skin) exposure routes with specific first aid measures
All three chemicals have ocular (eye) exposure routes with water flushing protocols
All three chemicals list ingestion as an exposure route with specific guidance (e.g., 'DO NOT INDUCE VOMITING')
Swallow is synonymous with ingestion; both refer to oral exposure to chemicals
HTTP 200 response confirmed; website is publicly accessible and operational

pi_items.verified_value: NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, Office of Response and Restoration)
pi_items.source: Confirmed through CAMEO Chemicals about page and web searches

privileged_info_summary: The CAMEO Chemicals website (cameochemicals.noaa.gov) is a real, publicly accessible NOAA database for hazardous materials information. All three target chemicals are real substances with authentic first aid data: Hydrofluoric Acid first aid includes water flushing and calcium gluconate gel application; Sodium Cyanide treatment includes amyl nitrite breathing and sodium thiosulfate administration, with hydroxocobalamin as the modern FDA-approved antidote; Phenol requires water or polyethylene glycol decontamination. All four exposure routes (inhalation, skin contact, eye contact, ingestion) are standard for hazardous chemicals. The metadata keywords for HF (calcium, gluconate) and sodium cyanide (amyl nitrite, thiosulfate, hydroxocobalamin) directly match CAMEO/medical guidance. However, phenol treatment keywords (polyethylene glycol, isopropyl, glycerol) appear in institutional safety protocols rather than being explicitly stated on the CAMEO Chemicals phenol datasheet itself, which primarily recommends water flushing. Data is non-synthetic, derived from real public sources.
